text:
One day a student moved to SF. He went to his Master and asked, "Teacher, I am new here and understand that SF is a city of many freedoms. What musnt I do?"

The teacher replied simply: "everything is permitted"

Hearing this, the student went into the city.

Growing weary from walking, the student stops to rest on a swing. Before he can sit down, the police come and remove the swing.

Filled with indignity, the student returns to his teacher and asks: "teacher, why did the police remove this thing which harmed no soul?"

His teacher replied simply: "everything is permitted"

At that moment, the student was enlightened

text: The value of thinkpiece/op-ed writers like MattY is that they take the distributed wave-function of ambient takes, and collapse it into Takes. This can be annoying (YIMBYs all saying the same things), but ultimately I think its probably necessary for discourse to have any impact

raw.full_text: The value of thinkpiece/op-ed writers like MattY is that they take the distributed wave-function of ambient takes, and collapse it into Takes. This can be annoying (YIMBYs all saying the same things), but ultimately I think its probably necessary for discourse to have any impact

text:
For example: this collapsing has made YIMBY groups much more effective than they'd otherwise be, both by making their policy pushes more co-ordinated, but also by make the terms of the debate clearer, so that people can argue over specific facts vs broad vibes. 

"Does making new housing look nicer reduce opposition to new housing" is an empirical question that its useful for YIMBYs to have truth-seeking debates over. People like MattY get everyone on the same page that:
1) This is a debated claim and
2) Here are the useful axes on which the claim should be debated

Then everyone can be having the same discussion and thus make a lot more progress towards consensus

text:
Thoughts on Scott's "Your own moon" post:

I didn't think it was very good? Mainly, it made a few assumptions that I disagree with
 
1) AGI world means ~instant post-scarcity

Given current trajectories, I think its likely that my whole life, and probably that of my children, will not be in true post-scarcity. Even if we get pretty aggressive TAI, it will be "10x or 100x industrial revolution" not "instantly tiles the lightcone with CEV". 

2) Being subject to the whims of an oligarch is good

The post assumes that "being given a bunch of nice stuff" by one of the oligarchs who controls all resources is just as good as being the oligarch (or being in a non-oligarchy). Even if you are given enough stuff, your right to possess *anything* is subject to their judgement. Maybe we get lucky and Dario is just really nice and becomes immortal and never abuses this? But fearing that he'd say, ban the consumption of meat amongst his dependents, and thus restrict everyone's freedom with no input from them, seems pretty reasonable. 
Maybe the AGIs will prevent this kind of abuse? But in that world there are no Oligarchs, there is just the AGI monarchy, and we hope CEV is good. Scott's post implicitly assumes that there will be people controlling the AGI, and that those people are the people who run the labs. At the very least, he assumes that AGI will respect property rights and lead to those people getting outsized resources for "reasons"? Unclear to me how that happens unless they have control over what the AGI does.

text:
Talking to @__venki__ I think maybe this gets closer to the crux, though still not perfectly stated: 
1) what future are the people who talk about needing to make money now talking about

I think these people are mostly worried about “AI destroys the labor market but doesn’t allow for self-replicating production robots or whatever”. You can debate over whether that’s a reasonable future to worry about, but the post doesn’t. It assumes that AI will be ~maximally transformative short of paperclipping, which I don’t think is the scenario the “I need to be Winning harder guys” are talking about 

2) to the extent it *is* sometimes what people are talking about, I think it’s because they want to be one of the oligarchs shaping the future, or at least to get a vote in how AGI is wielded, vs relying on the largess of others

text:
@amandaorson Hard numbers:

Bill Gates owns ~275,000 acres [1]
Net Worth ~$118B [2]
Benchmark land value $5,830/acre on the higher end [3]

$5,830/acre * 275,000 acres ≈ $1.60B
$1.60B / $118B ≈ 1.4% of Bill Gates' net worth is land

text:
I usually find myself firmly in the Cowenite sphere in terms of policy, but one major diversion is around how much paternalism is appropriate.

I was listening to the recent CwT and he pushes on this level of "people are going to do dumb things anyway, you should just let them"
[1]

I often hear stuff from econ-sphere folks in this vein. Now it seems obviously bad for lots of people do gamble, or do life-destroying drugs, or whatever. I do think people disagree that you can't really have a functional society where everyone is addicted to heroin. I think the econ claim is something like "people will do it anyway, and the regulations to stop it will have costly externalities" + "social antibodies will form, people will just know not to do the self-destructive things"

My counter here is that regulating to ban or sin-tax things is part of the social antibodies being formed! No society ever relied purely on self-control to protect people from self-destructive vice. Its also clearly true that prohibition works! Unbanning weed caused people to smoke a lot more weed, even if people were smoking non-0 weed before it was legalized.

Historically people did this sort of enforcement this more informally and through non-state actors, and maybe econ folk would prefer that? However, that relies on a world where non-government entities can enact penalties on you. See my earlier post about the Taiping era and Opium/fiscal prudence:
https://t.co/nMoOpRl0fH

We intentionally stopped doing this though. Liberal democratic society intentionally disempowered non-state entities from having that kind of control over adults. There are very few ways for anyone besides the government to restrict your behavior. If I am an alcoholic and don't want to be tempted by alcohol, I don't have a family clan that controls my finances and can freeze my bank account if I buy liquor.
The only real option I have is The State if I want my community to be able to pre-commit to reducing the opportunities for self-destructive mistakes amongst our members. 

Its correct to observe that the state is a blunt instrument and regulations on vice can have high costs, but I think this vibe of shrugging and say "oh well, people should just have more self-discipline" feels very ahistorical in terms of how societies have come to flourish or flounder.

That can’t explain a ~15-pt never-married gap

text:
fwiw I gave the upper bound by my math here

Modal estimate: ~1 percentage point of the ~15-pp SF–NYC “never-married by 40” gap (≈7%), plausibly ~0–2 pp. Back-of-envelope: SF has ~4.7 pp more MSM than NYC’s core county (18.5% vs 13.8%) , and gay men’s marriage rate is ~⅓ straight men’s (18.2 vs 54.7 marriages per 1,000 unmarried)  -> even if that translated to ~30-pp higher “never-married by 40,” you get 0.047×0.30≈1.4 pp, then haircut (MSM≠gay; gay men do marry) → ~1 pp.

text:
Every time I go to Legion of Honor, it makes me meditate on some new facet

Yesterday: The discontinuity in Western art styles in the 20th century. You spend hours walking through 900 years of stylistic evolution. Things slowly evolve and change, you see techniques and aesthetics and symbols layering century after century. Then you get to 1900s modernity and its just a complete shattering of every aesthetic norm seemingly out of nowhere.

I still feel like I don't deeply understand What The Hell Happened even after having various pro/anti people explain their takes on this to me.

Agree with this. The essay in question presumes that AI drives massive deflation (plausible!), but if there's a bunch of deflation, then the government printing money and handing it to people doesn't really risk high inflation?

I think the bigger problem here is presumably that these shift in labor value change political calculus a lot/etc, not that they cause some mundane employment shock

text:
Wealth correlates with age in every society that has ever existed. You spend 40 years accumulating and then draw down. "Over-60s own half of UK wealth" doesn't say much imo

I agree it's genuinely unclear what fraction of pension spending is redistribution versus forced savings. UK state pensions are PAYG, yes. But PAYG isn't automatically redistributive: it depends on whether each generation gets roughly the same deal. If they do, it's just savings with extra steps. 

The question is how much the deal is deteriorating, and I think the answer is "some, for a mix of demographic and policy reasons."

The triple lock has been generous. Council tax is based on 1991 valuations. Planning restrictions are a wealth transfer to incumbents. These are all real and fair targets. But even without any of them, the dependency ratio alone would be straining the system, and obviously not an easily foreseeable policy flaw.

text:
I think Jan Morris’s HK book is an interesting lens with which to think about Dubai or other “fast growing rootless” cities

Some bits:
- many of the criticisms of cultural shallowness applied to early HK
- she claims this is to some substantial extent because of high immigration + low births. First gen people just care about winning, 2nd/3rd gen people care about culture (and are better at it)
- everyone thought HK was going to implode when opium trade ended, and again when Shanghai outpaced it as a port. Didn’t happen 
- HK also never had inclusive political institutions really. Governorship -> Chief Exec
- but it did have way more naturalization than Dubai does
- lots more I can’t fit here

text:
I think Arendt is almost exactly wrong about everything in such an intense way that its actually informative

- Arendt thinks the Greeks had it right: household = necessity, polis = freedom/speech/showing up as a distinct person among equals. Her complaint is that modernity let household logic (economics, managing needs) eat the public realm.

- But social media is basically her ideal? People appearing through pure speech, competing for recognition, no violence or coercion possible. Twitter is even more Arendtian than the agora

- Anonymous Poasters seem like the perfect example. An account like @tszzl has no body, no household, no institutional role. A recognizable self built entirely out of public speech. This happened inside exactly the conditions that supposedly prevent it!

- Her arrow points the wrong way. She predicted modernity would get more material and managerial. Instead things became ultra post-material. “Touch grass” is an anti-Arendtian injunction, Trump is an intensely symbolic figure.

- To the extent something is wrong, it feels like the actual crisis is the inverse: the political colonizing the household. The space where you’re loved rather than evaluated is dissolved by the harsh light of Commentary.

- Elon is a perfect case here. solved the household problem completely, walked into the appearance machine, got destroyed by it. In her framework he should be flourishing. He’s obviously not!

- The private realm isn’t what you escape from. It’s what keeps you whole. She has no framework for this because she thinks the household is just where you handle necessity. I think she would have been a better predictor if she had been either more Christian or more Chinese

- Anonymous Poasters seem like the perfect example. An account like @tszzl has no body, no household, no institutional role. A recognizable self built entirely out of public speech. This happened inside exactly the conditions that supposedly prevent it!

- Her arrow points the wrong way. She predicted modernity would get more material and managerial. Instead things became ultra post-material. “Touch grass” is an anti-Arendtian injunction, Trump is an intensely symbolic figure.

- To the extent something is wrong, it feels like the actual crisis is the inverse: the political colonizing the household. The space where you’re loved rather than evaluated is dissolved by the harsh light of Commentary.

- Elon is a perfect case here. solved the household problem completely, walked into the appearance machine, got destroyed by it. In her framework he should be flourishing. He’s obviously not!

- The private realm isn’t what you escape from. It’s what keeps you whole. She has no framework for this because she thinks the household is just where you handle necessity. I think she would have been a better predictor if she had been either more Christian or more Chinese

text:
Some dialectics on AI progress:

A) What if we make AI systems that do more explicit reasoning, like causal modeling. We could get better capabilities when answering hard questions, and more transparency

B) But what about the bitter lesson? Isn't it the case that all investment into systematizing good reasoning is a mistake, since we'll just RL harder to solve these things?

A) It seems to be like the bitter lesson applies well for domains where you're targeting human-parity or sub-human performance, since there's lots of training data to work off of. It's unclear to me how to get lots of training data for something like "making correct statistical inferences over complex real-world systems", 

B) Sure maybe there's not tons of data for pretraining, but surely we can set up some kind of RL environment?

A) Its really not clear to me what that would look like. As you get into harder and harder real-world problems, rewards just become so sparse. AI progress will probably continue to go exponential for math and programming because its easy to generate lots of dense reward, but for something like "figure out where the best place to open a new mine is", I don't know how you'd get enough signal to RL off of. Maybe this is why the Eliezer claims about intelligence->superintelligence curves seem off? Human intelligence wasn't bottlenecked by calories, but because the rewards to greater intelligence just become exponentially more sparse at some point. Its notable that the models are often bad at stats code, and also that stats code is often a case where the only way to validate correctness is to read the code and reason over it. There is no way to look at only the output and know whether its correct, until you observe reality which might take a very long time, or involve an expensive experiment. 

B) That makes sense when talking about super empirical problems, but you're claim is that we should sit down and design some causal modeling harness? If one believes that G exists, then RL-ing super hard on increasingly difficult programming and math and games (where reward is dense), should just increase General Intelligence in a way that transfers to arbitrary domains, including things like correctly setting up and using causal models or deciding whether some stats code is correct.

A) So far it seems like we've seen less programming->other domains transfer learning than we'd expect? The models are still really bad at doing statistics in robust ways (for example, they're extremely susceptible to confirmation bias). It seems unclear whether doing more/harder RL on math/programming will teach them how to be good at reasoning under uncertainty, because to some degree the domains which have dense rewards are also the domains with less uncertainty?

B) Correctly debugging software involves many of the same skills as solving scientific questions or acting under uncertainty as a business. You have to come up with theories, consider the EV of pursuing different solutions, avoid confirmation bias, etc. Why shouldn't we believe it will eventually teach general rules of good reasoning?

A) It still fundamentally teaches the models to be multi-shot. Claude while programming has a tendency to be a "hacker". It will try many ideas until something works. This is great for math/programming, when you can just spend more inference compute and do more experiments, but many/most problems in the world are 1-shot! You just have to think really really hard, do some modeling maybe, and then get it right the first or second time. 

B) Again, what about that is hard to RL for in a programming or math sim? 

A) I guess the core here is whether you think that will transfer from a math sim to something like being asked to decide whether a given statistical model accurately describes the world. Seems reasonable to believe this! But also, it seems like actively pursuing "how can we teach the models these skills" will probably go faster than "just chuck the models harder and harder SWE problems". There are some skills that you can probably only learn by having to do certain kinds of 0-shot/"unverifiable" tasks.

text:
Re: People like Sam Kriss complaining about the Bay Area Scene:
- Urbanites have been snootily writing articles about how they're Not Like The Other scenesters since at least the 1700s, see Samuel Johnson: "The greatest part of mankind have no other reason for their opinions than that they are in fashion."
- This is just an inevitable feature of having an intellectual scene. If lots of people are talking to each-other a lot, then they'll end up speaking a similar language and following similar trends. This doesn't prevent them from generating real insight! It's plausibly true that The Romantics were in some sense more conformist than randomly selected proletariat across Europe, but they were still doing something novel
- One framing here is that its possible to be both heterodox (relative to the larger cultural sphere) but still internally have a lot of similarity, even when there's lively internal debate. Especially when viewed from the outside, where differences blur together
- Subcultures can and do become navel-gazing, so some amount of critique here is fine and good. I just think that you shouldn't use it to dismiss a scene outright. Large groups of people are almost always dumb in some ways but effective in others.

text:
fwiw I think it's necessary to acknowledge that, unlike ~every major Christian denomination, many (most) interpretations of Islam don't recognize a distinction between secular and religious domains. 

The New Testament is strikingly uninterested in governance, whereas the Quran really does legislate directly on things like inheritance, contracts, and criminal punishment, and assumes Islamic law as a thing that has legal force. That means your version of assimilation does rule out people getting to keep their religions as-is. Muslims will have to accept a "liberalized" version of the faith. 

Muslims have of course done so in many places and times over the centuries, but I think it's genuinely gong to be harder than it would be for, say, Buddhists.

text:
‘Britain is a miserable sight. A society of failures, full of apathy, and aroused only by envy at the success of others. That is why we will continue to decline. Not because of our economic and industrial problems. They are soluble. But because the psychology of our people is in such an appalling – I fear irretrievable – state. Meanness has replaced generosity. Envy has replaced endeavour. Malice is the most common motivation … This is the social personality of a loser’.

Hard to imagine a senior official talking about their own country this way today. Trump’s American carnage speech is maybe similar, but much more “external locus of control” vs “yeah all my constituents are losers”

text:
A thing I believe wrt techno-optimism/pessimism: one reason why we see technology as mostly having been good is that we’re the cultural and genetic descendants of the victors, or those which adapted to fit the new technology 

It seems definitely true that whole swathes of potentiality and culture have been wiped out ~irrecoverably by technology (even going back to agriculture). If you could show the losers the future I do not think they would find it self-evidently good. 

What one does with this fact is up for debate though. I think “I’m an inheritor of the Technological Man, and it’s my culture to keep reaching for the stars even if some things are lost in the process” is a pretty defensible position.

Also, I have some faith that solving ~all material constraints gives us a lot of leeway to support a “let a billion flowers bloom” attitude towards the cultures of the future.

For my sake, I’m quite happy to be on this side of the event horizon, even if I do morn for what was lost and appreciate those sacrifices. How can I say it was wrong for us to have changed like we did? I may as well regret my own birth! I can do no such thing, life is too full of joy for that.

text:
Everyone is reading a lot about datacenter backlash and assuming this means the datacenters don't get built. If so, why are huge datacenter projects like Stratos happening in the US instead of internationally?

I think it's worth considering fracking as an analogue: fracking is an environmentally intensive industrial activity with a lot of resistance/backlash, but in practice the US does a huge amount of fracking anyway!

It turns out there are a lot of states/counties, they have a fair degree of independence, and the US is actually pretty good at building out big capital-intensive projects (as long as they don't happen near cities)

text:
I think one of my primary learnings for this year is that of taking “brace/embrace” much more seriously in many domains.

Some examples:
- pickleball: if I try to white-knuckle when I’m tired, I place worse than if I try to just have a good time
- flying: the moment I stop conceptualizing my goal on the plane as “pass the time quickly”, and instead just enjoy the plane experience for what it is, I have a way better time

text:
Hannah Arendt’s framing of Labor vs Work (though I resonate more with Labor vs Artifice) is much clearer than Labor Theory of Value or Bullshit Jobs, as a potential lens for “why are people unhappy with their work even when they have ~objectively good jobs compared to the past”

Jobs where your work doesn’t produce anything that lasts are uncompelling.  I want to see the mark my being leaves on the world. Either via Artifice, or via relationships and changed lives. I do think “disposable software” à la vibecoding makes this software engineering less fulfilling in this way. When I worked in a grocery store, this was what made it soul-eroding. I would move boxes every day knowing that I was just a cell in the metabolism of the store. Mankind naturally strives to be more than a channel through which value flows. People want to be shaping something new!

text: I think the “metabolism” framing feels extremely compelling. Every time I read the book I hate it, because her writing is so misaligned with the linguistic shape of my brain, and then a week later I’ll find myself unable to stop thinking about it. I guess this is the mark of an excellent writer.

text:
A think @__venki__ pointed out to me once that has really stuck with me: industry % of GDP is an approximate proxy for industry employment 

In some sense this is obvious, but I feel like it does seem Of Note to me. For example, healthcare is ~18% GDP, ~12% of jobs
